title: QUACOD: Quantum Optimization via Coordinate Descent for Scalable Drone Scheduling
authors: Van-Quang-Huy Nguyen, Hoang-Quan Nguyen, Samee U. Khan, Ilya Safro, Khoa Luu
abstract: Quantum computing has demonstrated its potential to solve various optimization problems, including drone scheduling, which is important not only for drone delivery but also for logistics in general. However, one of the main obstacles is that practical drone scheduling settings typically require quantum resources that current hardware cannot provide. Therefore, in this work, we introduce a new Quantum Optimization via Coordinate Descent (QUACOD) approach to address this problem under the constraint of a limited number of available qubits. By leveraging coordinate descent, QUACOD decomposes the original high-complexity problem into multiple subproblems, which are then solved using quantum optimization. In our experiments, QUACOD outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) quantum-based drone scheduling method not only in optimized drone completion times but also in scalability, handling up to 5 times more drones and 35 times more routes. In addition, QUACOD demonstrates that hardware-efficient circuits are effective for optimization problems. Together, these contributions advance quantum computing toward practical applications in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era.
summary: This paper introduces QUACOD, a new hybrid quantum-classical algorithm designed to tackle large-scale optimization problems like drone scheduling. By using coordinate descent to break down complex problems into smaller, qubit-manageable subproblems, it achieves significantly better scalability and performance on NISQ-era hardware compared to existing methods.
main_problem: The challenge of solving large-scale, NP-hard drone scheduling problems on NISQ-era hardware, which lacks the necessary qubit count and quality for direct optimization.
main_result: The QUACOD algorithm outperforms the previous state-of-the-art (QUADRO) in both solution quality and scalability, handling up to 5 times more drones and 35 times more routes.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical approach using Coordinate Descent to decompose large problems into smaller subproblems, which are then optimized using Variational Quantum Algorithms (VQE or QAOA) with hardware-efficient circuits.
model_or_system: The problem is modeled as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem, which is mathematically equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising model Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Drone completion times (makespan) and the objective function value during the optimization process.
important_parameters: Number of drones (q), number of routes (n), recharging time (c), number of available qubits (m), and the penalty parameter (p).
main_assumptions: Drones are assumed to be identical, routes are pre-determined, and the model does not account for heterogeneous vehicle fleets or complex real-world logistics.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the drone scheduling problem; Fig 2 depicts the hybrid quantum-classical loop; Fig 3 illustrates the heuristic for minimizing completion time; Fig 4 shows the hardware-efficient ansatz; Fig 5 shows the convergence of the objective function.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the drone scheduling problem, presents the QUACOD algorithm based on coordinate descent, describes the QUBO reformulation and VQE implementation, details the experimental setup and benchmarks against QUADRO, and concludes with scalability results and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Illustration of the drone scheduling problem with a charging time of 1.15 hours between routes. (a) Three predetermined routes, A, B, and C, have completion times of 3.4, 2.8, and 4.4 hours, respectively. (b) An example schedule assigns routes A and B to the red drone (completion time: 3.4 + 1.15 + 2.8 = 7.35 hours) and route C to the green drone (completion time: 4.4 hours). The objective is to minimize the time until all drones have completed their routes, which equals 7.35 hours in this example.

Fig. 2. The hybrid quantum-classical optimization loop of a variational quantum algorithm. The quantum component prepares a parameterized state |ψ(θ)⟩and measures the expected value ⟨Hf⟩, which the classical optimizer uses to iteratively update θ until convergence.

Fig. 3. Heuristic for transformation from Cmax minimization to con- vergence toward T/q. (a)-(c) Route assignment minimizes Cmax toward the optimal solution. (d)-(e) Minimizing the sum of squared distances P j∈[q]  Tj −T

Fig. 4. A single-layer hardware-efficient ansatz for an m-qubit register, comprising two Ry rotation blocks with a linear CZ entanglement chain on consecutive qubit pairs in between. Each Ry gate has its own trainable parameter, giving 2m parameters θ0,1, . . . , θ0,m, θ1,1, . . . , θ1,m in total.

Fig. 5. Convergence process of QUACOD on the Augerat dataset [29] adapted for the drone delivery problems [30]. Column (A) shows the objective function value per iteration; Column (B) shows the time (in hours) for all drones to complete all routes per iteration.
